name: Thamnophilidae
description: Antbirds are a highly diverse family of Neotropical passerines that includes antshrikes, antwrens, and antbirds. Many species are obligate followers of army ant swarms.

children.name: Taraba
children.description: Taraba is a monotypic genus of large antshrike with a heavy hooked bill and prominent crest.

children.name: Taraba major
children.description: The great antshrike is the largest species in the antbird family, found from southern Mexico to Argentina. Males are black above and white below with a black breast band.

children.name: Thamnophilus
children.description: Thamnophilus is the largest genus of antshrikes, with over 30 species distributed across the Neotropics.

children.name: Thamnophilus doliatus
children.description: The barred antshrike is a boldly patterned bird with black-and-white barring, found from Mexico to northern Argentina. It inhabits thick undergrowth and forest edges.

children.name: Willisornis
children.description: Willisornis is a genus of antbird named in honor of ornithologist Edwin O'Neill Willis.

children.name: Willisornis poecilinotus
children.description: The common scale-backed antbird has a distinctive scaly appearance on its back and is found in Amazonian lowland forests. It regularly attends army ant swarms to catch fleeing prey.

children.name: Pithys
children.description: Pithys is a genus of antbird known for its spectacular white facial plumes and faithful army ant following behavior.

children.name: Pithys albifrons
children.description: The white-plumed antbird is a striking black bird with a brilliant white forehead and long white facial plumes. It is an obligate army ant follower in Amazonian rainforests.

children.name: Myrmoborus
children.description: Myrmoborus is a genus of antbirds typically found in lowland Amazonian forests, often near water.

children.name: Myrmoborus myotherinus
children.description: The black-faced antbird is a widespread Amazonian species with a distinctive black face mask in males. It favors dense understory vegetation near streams and forest edges.

children.name: Sciaphylax
children.description: Sciaphylax is a genus of small antbirds found in lowland Amazonian forests.

children.name: Sciaphylax hemimelaena
children.description: The chestnut-tailed antbird is a small, rufous-tailed species found in western Amazonia. It forages close to the ground in dense forest undergrowth.

children.name: Microrhopias
children.description: Microrhopias is a monotypic genus of small, active antwren with distinctive white wing spots.

children.name: Microrhopias quixensis
children.description: The dot-winged antwren is a small, hyperactive bird with white dots on its wings, found from southern Mexico to Amazonia. It moves actively through the canopy in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Cymbilaimus
children.description: Cymbilaimus is a genus of antshrikes with heavily barred plumage, found in lowland forests from Honduras to Amazonia.

children.name: Cymbilaimus lineatus
children.description: The fasciated antshrike is a boldly barred black-and-white bird with a heavy hooked bill, found from Honduras to the Amazon. It hunts large insects in the forest understory.

children.name: Batara
children.description: Batara is a monotypic genus containing the largest species in the antbird family, with a powerful bill and distinctive barred plumage.

children.name: Batara cinerea
children.description: The giant antshrike is the largest antbird, reaching 34 cm in length, with a massive hooked bill and bold black-and-white barring. It inhabits montane forests of the southern Andes.

children.name: Mackenziaena
children.description: Mackenziaena is a genus of large, striking antshrikes with ornate spotted and scalloped plumage, endemic to South America.

children.name: Mackenziaena severa
children.description: The tufted antshrike is a large, dark antshrike with white spots and a shaggy crest, endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, Paraguay, and Argentina.

children.name: Mackenziaena leachii
children.description: The large-tailed antshrike is a striking black antbird with white scalloping and a very long tail, found in the Atlantic Forest and southern Andes.

children.name: Dysithamnus
children.description: Dysithamnus is a genus of small, thick-set antbirds known as antvireos, found in forests from southern Mexico to Argentina.

children.name: Dysithamnus stictothorax
children.description: The spot-breasted antvireo is a small, olive-grey bird with white spots on the breast, endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil.

children.name: Dysithamnus mentalis
children.description: The plain antvireo is a small, drab olive-grey bird with a pale eye, found from Mexico to Argentina. It is the most widespread species in its genus.

children.name: Dysithamnus xanthopterus
children.description: The rufous-backed antvireo is a strikingly plumaged bird with a rufous back, grey head, and black wings with white spots, endemic to the Atlantic Forest of Brazil.

children.name: Myrmotherula
children.description: Myrmotherula is a large genus of tiny, active antbirds known as antwrens, many of which forage in mixed-species flocks in the forest canopy and understory.

children.name: Myrmotherula ignota
children.description: The moustached antwren is a tiny bird with a distinctive black moustachial stripe, found from eastern Panama to western Amazonia.

children.name: Myrmotherula brachyura
children.description: The pygmy antwren is one of the smallest antbirds, with a short tail and a black-and-white striped throat, found in Amazonian lowland forests.

children.name: Myrmotherula axillaris
children.description: The white-flanked antwren is a small, active bird with distinctive white flank patches, found from Honduras to Amazonia in lowland forests.

children.name: Myrmotherula longipennis
children.description: The long-winged antwren is a small grey antwren with white wing bars and a black throat in males, found in Amazonian terra firme forests.

children.name: Myrmotherula longicauda
children.description: The stripe-chested antwren has streaked underparts and a relatively long tail, found in mid-elevation forests along the eastern slope of the Andes.

children.name: Myrmotherula multostriata
children.description: The Amazonian streaked antwren is a small, heavily streaked antwren found in riparian and seasonally flooded forests across the Amazon Basin.

children.name: Myrmotherula surinamensis
children.description: The Guianan streaked antwren is a small, streaked black-and-white antwren of swampy forests in the Guianas and northeastern Amazonia.

children.name: Sclateria
children.description: Sclateria is a monotypic genus of medium-sized antbird with distinctive silver-grey plumage, found in swampy forests of Amazonia.

children.name: Sclateria naevia
children.description: The silvered antbird has a striking silver-grey head and breast with white spots on the wings, inhabiting swampy and flooded forests across the Amazon Basin.

children.name: Percnostola
children.description: Percnostola is a genus of medium-sized antbirds found in lowland forests of northern Amazonia.

children.name: Percnostola rufifrons
children.description: The black-headed antbird has a black head and breast with rufous wings and back, found in lowland forests of the Guianas and northern Brazil.

children.name: Drymophila
children.description: Drymophila is a genus of medium-sized antbirds with streaked plumage, found in forests and bamboo thickets of South America.

children.name: Drymophila ferruginea
children.description: The ferruginous-backed antbird has a rich rufous back, black tail, and grey underparts with white streaks, endemic to the Atlantic Forest of Brazil.

children.name: Thamnomanes
children.description: Thamnomanes is a genus of medium-sized antshrikes with bluish-grey plumage, often found leading mixed-species foraging flocks in Amazonian forests.

children.name: Thamnomanes caesius
children.description: The cinereous antshrike is a grey antshrike with a black throat in males, found in lowland Amazonian forests. It is a core member of mixed-species understory flocks.

children.name: Thamnomanes schistogynus
children.description: The bluish-slate antshrike has uniform blue-grey plumage with a pale yellow eye, found in lowland forests of western Amazonia.

children.name: Thamnomanes saturninus
children.description: The saturnine antshrike is a dark grey antshrike with a black throat, found in lowland forests of south-central Amazonia.

children.name: Herpsilochmus
children.description: Herpsilochmus is a genus of small, active antwrens with distinctive black-and-white head patterns, found in forests and woodlands of South America.

children.name: Herpsilochmus atricapillus
children.description: The black-capped antwren has a black cap, white supercilium, and olive-brown back, found in lowland forests and woodlands of eastern and central South America.

children.name: Herpsilochmus motacilloides
children.description: The creamy-bellied antwren has a black crown, white eyebrow, and creamy underparts, found in montane forests of the eastern Andes in Peru and Bolivia.

children.name: Herpsilochmus sticturus
children.description: The spot-tailed antwren has white spots on the tail tips and a black crown, found in lowland forests of the Guianas and northern Amazonia.

children.name: Herpsilochmus stictocephalus
children.description: Todd's antwren has a black crown and white eyebrow with olive back and white-spotted tail tips, found in lowland forests of the Guianas and northern Brazil.

children.name: Formicivora
children.description: Formicivora is a genus of small, slender antwrens with long tails and distinctive white eyebrows, found in a variety of habitats across South America.

children.name: Formicivora grisea
children.description: The white-fringed antwren has a white supercilium and white edges on the wing coverts, found in dry forests and scrub of northern South America.

children.name: Formicivora erythronotos
children.description: The black-hooded antwren has a black head and breast with rufous back and white belly, endemic to lowland forests of southeastern Brazil. It is considered endangered.

children.name: Formicivora rufa
children.description: The rusty-backed antwren has rich rufous upperparts and white underparts with a long tail, found in dry woodlands and savannas across much of central South America.

children.name: Cercomacra
children.description: Cercomacra is a genus of medium-sized antbirds with slender bills and long tails, found in dense thickets and forest edges of the Neotropics.

children.name: Cercomacra cinerascens
children.description: The grey antbird has uniform grey plumage with a black throat in males, found in lowland Amazonian forests where it skulks in vine tangles and dense undergrowth.

children.name: Cercomacra nigricans
children.description: The jet antbird is almost entirely black with white wing bars, found in dense undergrowth and forest edges from Panama to northwestern South America.

children.name: Cercomacra carbonaria
children.description: The Rio Branco antbird has black plumage with white wing bars and a long tail, endemic to gallery forests of the Rio Branco region in northern Brazil. It is considered vulnerable.

children.name: Hypocnemis
children.description: Hypocnemis is a genus of medium-sized antbirds with musical, warbling songs and streaked plumage, found in lowland Amazonian forests.

children.name: Hypocnemis cantator
children.description: The spotted antbird has white spots on the wing coverts and a black breast band, found in lowland forests of the Guianas and northeastern Amazonia.

children.name: Hypocnemis hypoxantha
children.description: The yellow-browed antbird has a distinctive yellow eyebrow and streaked underparts, found in bamboo thickets and forest edges of western Amazonia.

children.name: Hylophylax
children.description: Hylophylax is a genus of small, spotted antbirds with distinctive white-spotted wing coverts, found in lowland forests of South America.

children.name: Hylophylax naevius
children.description: The spot-backed antbird has white spots on the back and wing coverts with a black throat, found in lowland Amazonian forests where it often attends army ant swarms.

children.name: Hylophylax punctulatus
children.description: The dot-backed antbird has fine white dots on the back and a rufous crown, found in swampy and seasonally flooded forests across the Amazon Basin.
